today_picks.headline: Home underdog in a must-win Game 4 of the Eastern Conference Finals.
today_picks.the_thesis:
Montreal is down 2-1 in the Eastern Conference Finals after losing two straight overtime games in Carolina. Tonight they're home at the Bell Centre, where they've been dominant this postseason — they beat Tampa Bay in 7 games and Buffalo in 7 games to get here, with several of those wins coming at home.

The Hurricanes are the better team on paper, but playoff hockey is about momentum and home ice. Montreal won Game 1 decisively (6-2) and has shown they can compete with Carolina. At +130, we're getting 43% implied probability on a team that I think wins closer to 48% of the time.

The key players to watch are Cole Caufield and Nick Suzuki for Montreal, and Andrei Svechnikov and Sebastian Aho for Carolina. The Canadiens' goaltending has been solid, and the Bell Centre crowd will be electric for a must-win game.
today_picks.the_market: Montreal opened at +126 and has moved to +130 at DraftKings. The public is on Carolina, but the sharps know home ice matters in playoff hockey.
today_picks.weather_park: Bell Centre, indoor — no weather impact.
today_picks.case_against: Carolina has been the better team in Games 2-3, winning both in overtime. The Hurricanes' depth and defensive structure have been elite this postseason. Montreal's goaltending could crack under pressure.
today_picks.what_were_betting_on: Bell Centre crowd lifting Montreal, Caufield and Suzuki finding their scoring touch, and the Canadiens forcing a Game 5 with a 3-2 or 4-3 win.
today_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two OT losses at home? The Bell Centre owes Montreal one.

Gerrit Cole makes his 2026 debut tonight after recovering from Tommy John surgery. His rehab numbers were solid (4.66 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 28:3 K:BB across 29 innings), and he's facing a Royals team that ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (3.84). Noah Cameron (4.72 ERA, 1.45 WHIP) has been inconsistent, but the Yankees' offense has been potent (4.96 R/G).

The concern is Cole's limited sample — just 6.0 IP this season — and the fact that he'll likely be on a pitch count. But the Royals' offense is soft enough that even a short Cole outing (4-5 IP) should keep the game low.

today_picks.what_were_betting_on: Cole going 4-5 innings with 2 or fewer runs, wind suppressing power, and the game finishing 5-3 or 4-2.

Andrew Abbott (3.97 ERA, 1.46 WHIP) has been inconsistent, and his 10.5% BB rate creates baserunners.

Citi Field is a pitcher-friendly park (0.97 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF), and the wind blowing out to RF at 11 mph boosts HR by about 16% — but that helps the Mets' power hitters more than the Reds'.

The key concern is Brazobán's limited sample (26 IP) and the fact that he's averaging just 8.67 IP per start in his last 3. If he exits early, the Mets' bullpen will be tested.

today_picks.headline: Two elite starters, soft offenses, and a market that has overcorrected from 7.5 to 9.0.
today_picks.the_thesis:
Casey Mize and José Soriano are both pitching at elite levels this season, and the market has inexplicably pushed this total from 7.5 at open to 9.0. Mize owns a 2.47 ERA with a 0.98 WHIP and 25.1% K rate — he's been the best version of himself since returning from his adductor strain, posting a 2.43 ERA with a 0.98 WHIP across 43.2 innings. Soriano has been equally dominant with a 2.44 ERA and 27.9% K rate, though his 10.2% BB rate introduces some variance.

The offenses here are soft. Detroit ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (3.87), and the Angels aren't much better at 4.13. Comerica Park is a neutral-to-pitcher-friendly venue with a 0.96 runs park factor, and the wind is blowing left-to-right at 9 mph — not a HR-boosting environment.

The 1.5-run move from open to current is the kind of overcorrection that creates edge. The market is pricing in 9+ runs between two teams that average 8.0 combined against two starters with sub-2.50 ERAs. My fair line is 7.5-8.0, which gives us significant edge at 9.0.
today_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has climbed to 9.0 across all three books. This 1.5-run move is unusual for a game featuring two elite starters — the public is chasing runs that aren't coming.
today_picks.weather_park: Comerica Park, 71°F, wind 9 mph L-to-R — neutral conditions, no HR boost.
today_picks.case_against: Soriano's 10.2% BB rate could extend innings and put runners on base. If either starter exits early, both bullpens have been shaky. The Angels have scored 5+ runs in 3 of their last 7 games.
today_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, combined K rate suppressing contact, and the soft offenses failing to capitalize on the few baserunners they get.

all_picks.headline: Home underdog in a must-win Game 4 of the Eastern Conference Finals.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Montreal is down 2-1 in the Eastern Conference Finals after losing two straight overtime games in Carolina. Tonight they're home at the Bell Centre, where they've been dominant this postseason — they beat Tampa Bay in 7 games and Buffalo in 7 games to get here, with several of those wins coming at home.

The Hurricanes are the better team on paper, but playoff hockey is about momentum and home ice. Montreal won Game 1 decisively (6-2) and has shown they can compete with Carolina. At +130, we're getting 43% implied probability on a team that I think wins closer to 48% of the time.

The key players to watch are Cole Caufield and Nick Suzuki for Montreal, and Andrei Svechnikov and Sebastian Aho for Carolina. The Canadiens' goaltending has been solid, and the Bell Centre crowd will be electric for a must-win game.
all_picks.the_market: Montreal opened at +126 and has moved to +130 at DraftKings. The public is on Carolina, but the sharps know home ice matters in playoff hockey.
all_picks.weather_park: Bell Centre, indoor — no weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: Carolina has been the better team in Games 2-3, winning both in overtime. The Hurricanes' depth and defensive structure have been elite this postseason. Montreal's goaltending could crack under pressure.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Bell Centre crowd lifting Montreal, Caufield and Suzuki finding their scoring touch, and the Canadiens forcing a Game 5 with a 3-2 or 4-3 win.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two OT losses at home? The Bell Centre owes Montreal one.

all_picks.headline: Gerrit Cole's return, wind blowing in, and a total that's dropped from 9.0 to 8.5.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Gerrit Cole makes his 2026 debut tonight after recovering from Tommy John surgery. His rehab numbers were solid (4.66 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 28:3 K:BB across 29 innings), and he's facing a Royals team that ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (3.84). Noah Cameron (4.72 ERA, 1.45 WHIP) has been inconsistent, but the Yankees' offense has been potent (4.96 R/G).

The key edge here is the weather — wind is blowing in from LF at 9 mph, suppressing HR by about 12%. Kauffman Stadium already suppresses HR (0.92 PF), and the total has dropped from 9.0 to 8.5. We're getting even money on a line that's moved our way.

The concern is Cole's limited sample — just 6.0 IP this season — and the fact that he'll likely be on a pitch count. But the Royals' offense is soft enough that even a short Cole outing (4-5 IP) should keep the game low.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 9.0 and has dropped to 8.5. We're getting even money on the under at FanDuel — the sharps have already moved this line.
all_picks.weather_park: Kauffman Stadium, 82°F, wind 9 mph in from LF — 12% HR suppression, pitcher-friendly conditions.
all_picks.case_against: Cole's 6.0 IP sample is tiny, and he'll likely be on a pitch count (70-80 pitches). If he exits early, the Yankees' bullpen has been inconsistent. Cameron's 4.72 ERA could lead to a crooked inning.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Cole going 4-5 innings with 2 or fewer runs, wind suppressing power, and the game finishing 5-3 or 4-2.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Cole's back, the wind's in, and the sharps already moved this line. We're not early, we're on time.

all_picks.headline: Brazobán's 1.73 ERA at home against Abbott's 3.97 ERA.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Huascar Brazobán has been excellent in limited innings this season — 1.73 ERA with a 0.92 WHIP across 26 innings. His 0.35 HR/9 is elite, and he's facing a Reds team that ranks middle-of-the-pack in runs per game (4.5). Andrew Abbott (3.97 ERA, 1.46 WHIP) has been inconsistent, and his 10.5% BB rate creates baserunners.

The Mets are home favorites at -118, which implies about 54% win probability. My fair line is closer to 57%, giving us edge. Citi Field is a pitcher-friendly park (0.97 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF), and the wind blowing out to RF at 11 mph boosts HR by about 16% — but that helps the Mets' power hitters more than the Reds'.

The key concern is Brazobán's limited sample (26 IP) and the fact that he's averaging just 8.67 IP per start in his last 3. If he exits early, the Mets' bullpen will be tested.

all_picks.case_against: Brazobán's 26 IP sample is small, and his 8.67 IP/start average means the bullpen will be involved early. The Reds have power (Elly De La Cruz, Sal Stewart) that could capitalize on the wind.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Brazobán going 5+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, Mets offense capitalizing on Abbott's walks, and the game finishing 4-2 or 5-3 Mets.

The Braves offense is potent (5.25 R/G), but Early has been effective at limiting damage. The key is Elder dominating his half — if he goes 7 innings with 1-2 runs allowed, the under is in great shape even if Early gives up 3-4.

The key here is that Ohtani should dominate his half of the game, and the Dodgers' bullpen has been elite. Even if Sugano gives up 4-5 runs, Ohtani limiting the Rockies to 1-2 keeps this under.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Suarez's elite HR suppression anchoring the under, but the kill shot came from the wrong side of the matchup: Suarez himself collapsed. He sailed through four scoreless innings, then surrendered two runs in the fifth on Matt Olson's 15th homer (a two-run shot that tied it 2-2), and was chased without recording an out in the sixth after Austin Riley tripled, Eli White walked, and Michael Harris II delivered an RBI double — Suarez was charged with five earned runs on six hits over five-plus innings, walking three and hitting a batter, the worst outing of his season. Atlanta's three-run sixth made it 5-2, Harris added a two-run homer in the eighth off the Boston bullpen, and despite three Boston home runs (Duran, Rafaela, Kiner-Falefa), the Red Sox fell 7-6 — 13 combined runs, blowing past the 8.0 total. The model correctly flagged Strider's walk rate as a risk but misidentified which pitcher would blow up: Strider allowed only 3 runs in 5 innings and settled in after the first, while Suarez — the supposed anchor — had his worst start of the year. The thesis was built on the wrong pitcher's floor.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When anchoring an under on a specific starter's ERA/HR suppression, cross-check whether that pitcher has a recent 'cluster' of bad starts masked by a low season ERA — Suarez had allowed 12 of his 13 earned runs in just 13 innings across a subset of starts, a red flag that the ERA was misleading about his true variance profile; flag starters whose ERA and xERA diverge by >0.75 runs for under consideration (N=1, insufficient to establish rule).
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Suarez blowup does not confirm a systematic ERA-masking pattern, but the pre-game data point (12 of 13 ER in 13 innings of bad starts) was available and should have widened the confidence interval on his floor.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on two starters — Ashcraft held up his end completely (1 R, 6.1 IP, quality start), but the Cubs' half of the equation was a ghost. Jordan Wicks was not a real MLB starter: he had a career 5.21 ERA, had spent 2025 exclusively as a reliever with a 6.28 ERA in 14.1 innings, and was recalled from Triple-A Iowa on Sunday — the day before the game — only because Edward Cabrera went on the IL with a finger blister. The pack flagged 'thin data' on Wicks but still priced the under as if a competent starter was taking the mound. He was rocked for 8 runs on 9 hits in 4.1 innings: a Reynolds two-run double and a Valdez 415-foot two-run homer made it 5-0 in the first inning alone, and the game was effectively over before Ashcraft threw his second pitch. Final: PIT 12, CHC 1 — 11 of Pittsburgh's 13 runs came off Wicks and the Cubs' mop-up arms, not Ashcraft.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When the Cubs' (or any team's) listed starter is a same-day recall from Triple-A with a career MLB ERA above 5.00 and no MLB starts in the current season, treat the opposing half of the total as uncapped and do not play unders unless the total is set 1.5+ runs above the ace's expected contribution alone — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One emergency-callup implosion is noise, but the DATA failure here (no MLB 2026 stats existed for Wicks at pick time, and the pack explicitly flagged it) is a process gap worth tracking.

all_picks.headline: Kyle Harrison (1.77 ERA, 32.2% K-rate) vs. a Cardinals team that has dropped four of five — and you're getting plus money on the run line.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Kyle Harrison is the only pitcher in the National League with an ERA below 1.85 and a strikeout rate above 30%. His 1.77 ERA, 59:14 K:BB, and 32.2% strikeout rate across 45.2 innings are not a fluke — his xERA per Statcast sits at 3.43, which is still comfortably a career best and validates the performance. The Brewers' pitching lab has turned Harrison into a weapon: he scrapped his cutter, sharpened his slurve (batters are 2-for-22 against it with 7 Ks), and improved his changeup's vertical drop by 3.4 inches. In his last two starts against the Cubs and Padres, he allowed zero runs across 12 innings with 18 strikeouts and one walk.

The Cardinals enter this game having dropped four of five and facing Harrison for the first time in his career. Michael McGreevy (2.40 ERA, 5.5% BB) is a solid arm, but the Brewers offense (4.92 R/G, best in the NL Central) has the firepower to generate a multi-run cushion. Milwaukee has won six of Harrison's last six starts and is 13-4 in their last 17 games overall.

The run line at +120 on FanDuel is the ladder pick because it lands squarely in the even-money band and Harrison's floor is as high as any arm on the board. You're getting paid to take the team with the better pitcher, the better offense, and the better recent form. The implied probability at +120 is 45.5% — our fair line puts the Brewers winning by 2+ at roughly 53%, giving us nearly 8 cents of edge.

all_picks.headline: Schlittler (1.50 ERA) vs. Falter (9.82 ERA) — the most lopsided pitching matchup on the board.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Cam Schlittler is the best pitcher in baseball right now and tonight he faces the worst starting pitcher on the slate. Schlittler enters with a 1.50 ERA, 0.86 WHIP, and a 75:13 K:BB ratio across 66 innings — his xFIP and xERA both sit around 2.60, meaning the underlying metrics validate the surface numbers. He is not a mirage. His 29.6% strikeout rate and 5.1% walk rate create an elite run-prevention profile, and the Royals lineup (22-32, bottom-third offense) profiles as one of the worst matchups he could face. Kansas City's 22% projected strikeout rate against right-handed pitching and average chase rate of 30.2% means they won't punish him with discipline or loud contact.

On the other side, Bailey Falter has a 9.82 ERA and 2.86 WHIP across just 7.1 innings — he is a spot starter who will likely be pulled after two innings in a piggyback arrangement. The Yankees lineup features Aaron Judge (17 HR), Ben Rice (16 HR), and Cody Bellinger, all projected to see multiple at-bats against a pitcher who cannot throw strikes. The run differential in this matchup is extreme. The Yankees have won 12 straight against Kansas City, and tonight's pitching edge is the most pronounced of that entire streak.

The run line at -118 on FanDuel is the value play here. The ML is -200, which is too short for a straight bet under our rules. But -1.5 at -118 is a gift — the Yankees should win by multiple runs far more often than the implied 54% suggests when Schlittler is dealing and the opponent is running out a 9.82-ERA arm. This is the highest-conviction play on the board.

all_picks.headline: Petco Park, Vasquez at 2.96, Luzardo's 28% K rate — runs come at a premium tonight.
all_picks.the_thesis: Petco Park is one of the three most pitcher-friendly parks in baseball (0.93 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF). Randy Vasquez has been a quiet bright spot for the Padres at 2.96 ERA, 1.19 WHIP, 0.83 HR/9. Jesus Luzardo's 4.85 ERA is misleading — his 28.0% K rate and 6.4% walk rate point to a much better underlying pitcher (his xFIP would be in the mid-3s). When two arms who miss bats and limit walks pitch at a park that kills offense, totals trend under.\n\nBoth offenses are mediocre — SD at 4.06 R/G, PHI at 4.02 R/G. Neither lineup is going to bludgeon its way past pitching of this quality at this park. -115 implies 53.5% — we make the under closer to 55%. Edge is thin but the data lines up.
all_picks.the_market: Total 7.5 since open, under prices vary from -115 (DK) to -125 (BetMGM). DK is the clear best for the under.
all_picks.weather_park: Petco Park, mild SoCal weather, marine layer often suppresses fly balls — net under-leaning.
all_picks.case_against: Luzardo's ERA says he's giving up runs in bunches. If he runs into a 3-run inning, the under math gets ugly fast. Padres bullpen has been shakier than Vasquez and a late inning could blow the total. Both offenses have power bats (Tatis, Schwarber, Harper) who can change a total with one swing.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6 IP at 2-3 ER each, bullpens not coughing up a late frame, final score 4-3 or lower.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two arms who miss bats, one of the league's quietest pitcher's parks, two so-so offenses. The math says under.

all_picks.headline: Tatsuya Imai's 16.9% walk rate is the worst on the board tonight.
all_picks.the_thesis: Tatsuya Imai is a mess: 8.31 ERA, 1.79 WHIP, 16.9% walk rate, 2.11 HR/9 across 17.1 IP. He's averaging 5.7 IP per start, which means he's not even giving the Astros length.\n\nKumar Rocker is no ace (3.60 ERA, 19.1% K%) but he's a competent league-average arm at home in a neutral-to-slight-hitter's park. The gap between the two starters is the entire edge. At -126 FanDuel, implied is 55.7% — we make this closer to 61%. Houston's offense is solid but limited by their starter — Imai will either walk himself out of the game in 4 IP or hit-batsman his way into a 5+ ER inning. Rangers ML is the play.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis was directionally correct — Imai walked three of the first four batters he faced and loaded the bases in the first inning, exactly the meltdown scenario we modeled. But he escaped via a double play, then retired 16 of the final 17 Rangers he faced en route to six no-hit innings on 97 pitches. Relievers Steven Okert and debutant Alimber Santa finished the combined no-hitter — the first in MLB since 2024 — in a 9-0 Houston win. Rocker, the 'competent league-average arm' we backed, allowed four runs in five innings and took the loss. The pick lost not because the Imai walk-rate thesis was wrong (he walked four on the night), but because a historically rare event — a combined no-hitter thrown by a pitcher entering with an 8.31 ERA — intervened. Texas was no-hit while only striking out three times; they simply couldn't find a hit despite making contact all night.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, pure VARIANCE — a combined no-hitter is a ~0.3% per-game event; no rule can be derived from this outcome.

all_picks.headline: Yesavage 1.07 ERA, zero homers allowed; Janson Junk in the other dugout.
all_picks.the_thesis: Trey Yesavage's debut stretch reads like a video game: 1.07 ERA, 28.7% K rate, 0 home runs allowed across 25.1 IP. Tiny sample, sure, but every underlying stat is elite — he's missing bats and not yielding hard contact. Janson Junk is a back-end starter with a 5.07 ERA, a 17.2% K rate (well below average), and a 1.31 HR/9 against a Toronto lineup that punishes mistakes.\n\nML at -160 isn't enough juice headroom for our taste, but the runline at +135 is right in the sweet spot. With Yesavage likely keeping Miami under 2 runs and the Jays getting at Junk for 4+, a 2-run margin is more often than +135 implies (~42.5%). We make it ~49% — clean edge.
all_picks.the_market: Total 7.5, ML -160 across books, RL +135 at BetMGM is best of three. Numbers stable since open.
all_picks.weather_park: Rogers Centre, indoor — neutral park, no weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: Yesavage's 25 IP sample is thin enough that he could throw a clunker tonight. Junk has stretches where he throws strikes and Miami's offense (4.28 R/G) is competent enough to keep a one-run game. RL is always vulnerable to bullpen meltdowns late.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Yesavage 6+ IP at 1 ER, Toronto putting up 5+ on Junk and the Marlins bullpen, final margin 2+.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Yesavage hasn't allowed a homer in 25 innings. Janson Junk has allowed eight in 55. We know what we're doing here.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Yesavage being Yesavage — but this was the start where the small sample finally regressed. He allowed a career-high five runs on five hits across 6.2 innings, surrendering more earned runs in one outing than he had in his previous five starts combined. Meanwhile, Junk — the supposed piñata — held Toronto to one run on eight singles across five innings, walking nobody and winning for the first time since April 28. The model correctly identified the pitching gap but built a run-line bet on a 25-inning ERA that was always one bad start away from evaporating; the 'case against' section even flagged this exact scenario and was overruled. Compounding matters, Vladimir Guerrero Jr. sat out with an elbow injury, quietly removing Toronto's most dangerous bat from the lineup the model priced in.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Do not back a run-line favorite when the anchor starter has fewer than 40 IP in the current season AND the implied win margin requires both the starter to hold AND the opposing back-end arm to implode — two independent events that must co-occur; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the failure mode (tiny-sample ERA regression + key lineup scratch) is a known structural risk that the pick's own 'case against' section identified and dismissed.

all_picks.headline: Ben Brown's 2.09 ERA against Mlodzinski's 3.96 — best pitcher edge near even money.
all_picks.the_thesis: Ben Brown has been quietly excellent: 2.09 ERA, 0.98 WHIP, 26.0% K rate, 7.8% walk rate. That's a borderline ace line over 38.2 IP. Mlodzinski is a serviceable mid-rotation arm (3.96 ERA, 20.4% K%, 1.40 WHIP) but the WHIP gap is the giveaway — Brown is suppressing baserunners at nearly half the rate. PNC Park is pitcher-friendly (0.95 runs, 0.91 HR), which actually helps both arms but mutes Pittsburgh's already-modest offense more than it hurts a strikeout pitcher like Brown.\n\nThe Cubs offense (Busch, Crow-Armstrong, Happ, Swanson) is materially deeper than Pittsburgh's, and at -122 the implied price is 55% — we make this closer to 60%. Clean edge.
all_picks.the_market: ML opened -122 implied 55%, sits at -122 to -125 across 3 books. Stable, sharp money hasn't moved it but the price still has value at DK.
all_picks.weather_park: PNC Park, 74°F, light cross-breeze, HR factor 0.91 — net neutral, favors pitching.
all_picks.case_against: Brown's 38 IP is a thin sample for the 2.09 ERA — his peripherals (K%, BB%) say he's good, but not 2.09 good. If he regresses to his FIP, the gap closes. Pittsburgh at home with a solid Mlodzinski outing and the Cubs slipping into a quiet offensive night is the bust path.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Brown going 6 strong, Cubs scratching 3-4 runs off Mlodzinski and the soft Pirates pen.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Best price, best floor, near even money. The boys' ladder doesn't need fireworks.
all_picks.ladder_note: Highest floor on the board at a near-even-money price. Brown is the most dominant SP we can back without paying -200+ juice, and Pittsburgh's bottom-third offense limits the variance. Clean spot to double up.

autopsy.post_mortem: Ben Brown delivered exactly the gem the thesis demanded — he was dominant through his outing, with Brandon Lowe's RBI double in the 3rd being the only real damage Brown allowed. The Cubs tied it 1-1 on a Michael Busch solo HR in the 5th, and the game was still very much alive. The loss turned on a single at-bat in the 7th: reliever Trent Thornton surrendered a solo home run to light-hitting catcher Henry Davis, a low-leverage, low-probability outcome that handed Pittsburgh a 2-1 lead the Cubs never recovered from. The Cubs offense went 6-for-the-game with just one extra-base hit and stranded five runners in the first three innings alone — but the decisive run was a bullpen blowup against a bottom-of-the-order bat, not a starter failure or a lineup collapse.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The thesis (starter edge + lineup depth) was sound and nearly correct — this is a one-game bullpen variance event, not a signal.

all_picks.headline: Misiorowski's 39.3% K rate vs Liberatore's 1.58 HR/9 — and we get plus money on the runline.
all_picks.the_thesis: Jacob Misiorowski is having a Cy Young-caliber season — 1.89 ERA, 0.88 WHIP, and a 39.3% strikeout rate. To frame that K rate: anything above 30% is elite, 39% is borderline historic. He goes 6+ innings a start and his Brewers bullpen has been one of the league's best behind him.\n\nMatthew Liberatore comes in at a 4.70 ERA, 1.55 WHIP, and a 1.58 HR/9 — that homer rate is brutal and exactly what gets exposed against Milwaukee's 4.92 R/G offense at home. The Brewers ML at -220 is too juiced for our straight-bet cap, so the runline at even money is the way to capitalize. With Misiorowski likely keeping STL to 1-2 runs and MIL likely scoring 4+ on Liberatore, a 2+ run margin is in play more often than the +100 price suggests.
all_picks.the_market: Total 7.5, MIL -1.5 at +100 on FanDuel — DK and MGM are -107 and -110, so FanDuel is the clear best price. ML at -220 violates juice cap, runline becomes the play.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor at American Family Field, neutral park — no weather variable.
all_picks.case_against: Runlines die on one-run wins. If Misiorowski exits at 95 pitches in the 6th with a 3-1 lead and the Brewers pen gives up a late run, the under-the-spread outcome is annoying but real. Liberatore can grind out 5 IP of 3-run ball even on bad days.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Misiorowski 6+ IP, 1-2 ER; Milwaukee crossing 4+ on Liberatore; final margin 2+ runs.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Even money on the better team, the better pitcher, and the better bullpen. We'll take that math.

all_picks.headline: Wind 14 mph straight in from center at Wrigley with Arrighetti carrying a 1.50 ERA — the under does the work for us.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Wrigley with the wind blowing in is a different ballpark than Wrigley with the wind blowing out. Today's wind is 14 mph in from center field, and the pack puts the HR impact at roughly -18%. That neutralizes the one real fear in this game, which is Jameson Taillon's 2.87 HR/9 — an alarming number normally, but turned into a paper tiger when fly balls die at the warning track.

On the other side, Spencer Arrighetti is throwing a 1.50 ERA with a 0.25 HR/9 across 36 innings. The 13.8% walk rate is the wart, but walks don't score by themselves against the Cubs lineup at Wrigley in a 57-degree game with wind in. Even granting some regression to Arrighetti's run prevention, the run environment today caps total scoring. Total opened around 8.5 and the wind report has nudged it to 8.0 at most shops — that's the market agreeing with us, and we're still buying because the weather impact is large.

The risk is walks turning into a crooked inning before Arrighetti settles, but in a 57-degree wind-suppressed game, even crooked innings tend to be 3-run innings, not 5-run innings.

the_data.context: Elite run prevention, no HR risk

the_data.label: Taillon HR/9
the_data.context: Normally bad — neutralized by wind today

all_picks.the_market: Total opened near 8.5 and has been bet down to 8.0 across the three books as the weather firmed up. Sharp money agreeing with our angle — we're still in at -110 because the weather edge is real, not priced fully.
all_picks.weather_park: Wrigley Field, 57°F, wind 14 mph in from CF — large HR suppression baked into the run environment today.
all_picks.case_against: Arrighetti's 13.8% walk rate is a real problem and the Astros lineup can capitalize even at Wrigley. If Arrighetti walks the park in the third inning and Taillon gives up a couple of doubles before exiting, this can be 5-3 through five and the under is in trouble even with wind in. Bullpens are average on both sides, so any early exits scale the variance.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Arrighetti throwing 5+ controlled innings, the wind killing 2-3 would-be doubles or homers, and both bullpens holding serve in cold air.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: When the flag at Wrigley points the right way, you don't have to overthink it.
all_picks.slate_assessment: 15-game MLB board, one NBA playoff-adjacent game, one NHL. Pitching mismatches and weather are the main signals — Wrigley's wind blowing in and a few stark starter advantages do most of the work today.
all_picks.executive_summary: Quiet but workable Friday board — 15 MLB games plus one NBA and one NHL we're staying off of. The pitching mismatches do the talking: Cristopher Sánchez against a Guardians lineup that doesn't slug, Arrighetti at Wrigley with 14 mph blowing straight in from center, and deGrom against an Angels staff trotting out a pitcher with a 17 ERA across 3.2 innings. Three plays, all pitcher-driven. Ladder rides with Sánchez at home — best floor on the board.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Seventeen total games on the board — 15 MLB, one NBA (Spurs/Thunder), one NHL (Avalanche/Golden Knights). Pricing climate is normal, no obvious overpriced favorites distorting the menu, and a handful of clean pitching mismatches make this a pitcher's slate. We're staying off the NBA and NHL entirely — data_confidence on both is below 0.4 in the pack and we don't have ratings to build a real fair line. Discipline over volume.

The games that earned a hard look: Cubs/Astros at Wrigley with 14 mph wind blowing in from center and Arrighetti's 1.50 ERA against Taillon's 4.97 and 2.87 HR/9 — under angle obvious. Phillies/Guardians with Cristopher Sánchez (1.82 ERA, 29.9% K rate, 5.2% BB rate over 64.1 IP) at home against a Cleveland lineup that doesn't punish lefties. Rangers at Angels where deGrom (3.02 ERA, 31% K, 0.91 WHIP) draws Grayson Rodriguez, who has a 17.18 ERA across 3.2 innings of work and is essentially an opener-by-default. Padres/A's at Petco with two run-suppressing factors stacked. Giants/White Sox at Oracle Park with Davis Martin's 1.61 ERA also tempting for an under.

What got cut: Padres/A's under — Petco plays under but Buehler's 5.01 ERA and 1.35 WHIP undermines the SP side of the equation and we'd need confirmed totals pricing to clear 8 cents of edge; we don't. Giants/White Sox under — same problem, McDonald has only 19 IP on the year, sample too thin and data_confidence too low to trust. Brewers/Dodgers — Henderson at 18 IP is too small a sample to bet around. Yankees/Rays — no stat line on Cole in the pack, can't fair-price it. Red Sox/Twins — two lefty starters with strong numbers but both under 31 IP, the under is tempting but variance is high with shallow bullpens behind both.

Final three: Cubs/Astros Under (wind + Arrighetti dominance + Taillon's HR problem neutralized by weather), Phillies ML at home with Sánchez (clearest SP edge on the board against a middling Cleveland offense), and Rangers ML with deGrom against a replacement-level Angels starter. The ladder goes to Phillies — Sánchez is the most predictable arm in this group, Citizens Bank plays neutral-to-hitter-friendly but Cleveland's offense doesn't scare us, and the price should land in our even-money band. Rangers ML is the highest-conviction play overall but likely prices too short to ladder.

all_picks.headline: E-Rod is dealing at home, the Rockies are still the Rockies away from Coors.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Eduardo Rodriguez has quietly put together a really good season — 2.53 ERA, 1.26 WHIP, and a 0.68 HR/9 at Chase Field, which is impressive given Chase boosts HR (1.07 park factor). The lefty matchup against Colorado is the angle: the Rockies are a bottom-tier offense overall and significantly worse away from Coors Field, where their hitters are calibrated to thin air and big gaps.

Zach Agnos on the other side has a 5.59 ERA and a 15.6% K rate — he's a contact-allowing back-end starter. Normally that's an over signal, but the Diamondbacks lineup against a righty isn't a juggernaut and Chase tonight is indoors with the roof closed (it's a dome environment effectively, neutral conditions).

The edge here is E-Rod's 5-6 inning floor against a lineup that historically struggles on the road, plus Agnos being good enough to keep Arizona to 4-5 runs even on a rough day. 9.5 is a high number — we just need this to land at 9 or fewer.
all_picks.the_market: Total at 9.5, juice -105 to -110. The number is set high because Agnos is bad and Chase plays hot, but the market may be underweighting how poor Colorado's road offense has been.
all_picks.weather_park: Chase Field indoors, neutral environment — park slightly favors HR but the roof neutralizes any weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: Agnos can absolutely get blown up — Arizona's lineup against a 5.59 ERA righty is the obvious lean, and if Ketel Marte and Pederson get to him early, 6 runs by the 4th inning isn't crazy. Chase also gives up cheap HRs. If E-Rod has any off night, the under is cooked quickly.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: E-Rod through 6 giving up 2 or fewer, Colorado scoring 3 or less on the road, and Agnos limiting Arizona to 4-5 runs.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: E-Rod's been the quiet ace nobody talks about. Rockies on the road don't change that.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Quiet Thursday board — 7 MLB games headlined by Mize vs Cantillo at a cold, wind-blown Comerica, plus a tossup NBA game and an NHL matinee with thin data. No standout team edges, but two pitching/weather setups jump out.
all_picks.executive_summary: Thursday gives us a 7-game MLB slate with two pitching matchups that scream under. Casey Mize is dealing (2.47 ERA, 0.42 HR/9) and gets a 60°F Comerica with a 9 mph wind blowing in from left — HR impact down ~11%. Across the league, Cade Cavalli and David Peterson take the mound in DC with another in-blowing wind. Lefty Eduardo Rodriguez at home against a punchless Rockies lineup rounds out the card. The ladder rides with Mize Under — best floor on the board.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Seven MLB games on the board plus a Knicks-Cavs NBA game and a Hurricanes-Canadiens NHL matchup with data confidence so low (0.30 and 0.35) they're non-starters before we even look at numbers. Pricing climate is normal — no obvious mispriced sides, so the edges live in pitching matchups and weather, not sides.

The games that earned a hard look: Tigers-Guardians at Comerica (Mize is pitching at an ace level — 2.47 ERA, 0.42 HR/9 — into 60°F with wind blowing in from left at 9 mph, projected -11.7% HR impact), Mets-Nationals in DC (two below-average lefties in Peterson and a righty in Cavalli, both with HR/9 under 0.55, into another in-blowing wind), Diamondbacks-Rockies (E-Rod 2.53 ERA at home vs Zach Agnos 5.59 ERA — a clear pitching mismatch), Braves-Marlins (Strider back but with only 14.2 IP this year and a wild 17.2% BB rate — sample too thin to trust), and Cardinals-Pirates (Ashcraft's 2.89 ERA looks real but Dustin May at 5.00 is the question mark).

What got cut: Braves-Marlins — Strider's 14.2 IP this season is sample-size territory, and a 17.2% BB rate is a red flag we can't ignore. Cardinals-Pirates — no clear edge on either side, Ashcraft is good but the line will reflect that. Yankees-Blue Jays — Rodón has 8 total IP this year with a 21.1% walk rate, the data is unusable. Angels-A's — Soriano is sharp but his 10.9% BB rate caps the under (system rule: unders rejected if SP BB/9 trends high), borderline cut. NBA and NHL games sit below the 0.6 data confidence floor.

The three survivors: Tigers-Guardians Under (Mize + weather + park), Nationals-Mets Under (two HR-suppressed pitchers + wind), and Diamondbacks ML/Under as the clear pitching mismatch. The ladder goes to Mize Under — the floor is built on a stable ace, a park that already suppresses runs (0.96 PF), and weather doing additional work. That's three independent factors all stacking the same direction. Highest floor, near even money, no variance landmines.

all_picks.headline: Schlittler's been unhittable; Yesavage on a rookie pitch count.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Cam Schlittler is the story of the Yankee rotation — 1.35 ERA, 0.78 WHIP, 30.1% K rate, and a microscopic 0.30 HR/9 across 60 innings. He's averaging 20 IP per start over his last three. Even acknowledging some regression is coming, those underlying numbers are best-pitcher-in-baseball territory, and against a Toronto lineup that's been streaky he should suppress runs comfortably.

Trey Yesavage on the other side has a shiny 1.40 ERA but only 19.1 innings pitched — sample is genuinely thin. The bigger issue: 6.37 IP per start across his last three means the Jays are pulling him by the 5th inning, which exposes their middle relief. The Bronx is HR-friendly (park factor 1.14), which works against the under at the margin — but Yankee Stadium's effect on RHP-vs-RHP matchups is more muted.

The play is built on Schlittler's dominance. If he goes 7+ IP allowing 1 or 2 runs (his season norm), the Yankees only need to score 3-4 off Yesavage and the Toronto pen to push the under home. A 4-3, 3-2, or 4-2 game gets us there.
all_picks.the_market: Total sits at 7.5 across all three books — line opened there too. Juice is friendly at -104 to -106.
all_picks.weather_park: Yankee Stadium outdoor, HR factor 1.14 — mild upward pressure on the over, the biggest risk factor here.
all_picks.case_against: Yankee Stadium's short porch is the bear case — one cheap solo shot off Schlittler changes the math. Yesavage's sample is tiny, so his 1.40 ERA could be hiding a worse underlying profile, but it could also mean he's pitching fine and the Yankees lineup gets stymied. If both lineups hit the ball in the air, this park can produce a 5-4 game in a hurry.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Schlittler 7+ IP of 1-2 runs, Yesavage 5 IP of 2-3, no porch homers turning solo HRs into 3-run shots.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two sub-1.50 ERAs on the bump and the line is seven and a half. The market noticed too — we still like it.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Big 15-game MLB board with one NBA playoff game and one NHL game. Several standout pitching matchups headline the day, with the Sale-vs-Junk dynamic in Miami and Schlittler-vs-Yesavage at Yankee Stadium leading a pitcher-friendly slate.

all_picks.headline: Joe Ryan is a top-tier arm. Mike Burrows is not. Cold weather seals it.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Joe Ryan has been outstanding — 3.20 ERA, 1.01 WHIP, 25.6% K rate, and an elite 0.54 HR/9 across 50+ innings. That HR/9 is genuinely top-10 in baseball; he doesn't give up the big inning. With a 6.9% walk rate and a tidy WHIP, baserunners are scarce, and his last three starts have averaged 16.7 IP — he's pitching deep into games.

Houston counters with Mike Burrows, who's been hittable (5.72 ERA, 1.53 WHIP, 1.80 HR/9). That HR/9 number is brutal but it's partly Houston's home park; on the road at Target Field with a 54°F game-time temp, the ball doesn't fly. The Twins offense is league-average at best, but they don't need to drop 6 — they need to drop 4 against a struggling SP, which is well within reach.

The weather is the kicker. 54°F suppresses ball flight materially — cold air is dense air, fly balls die at the warning track. Combine that with Target Field's already-neutral park factors, Ryan's HR-suppression skill, and you have a low-scoring environment baked in.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened 8.0 and held at 8.0 across all three books — sharp consensus on the number. Juice is -109 to -110 on the under.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field outdoor, 54°F, light 6mph crosswind — cold weather is a meaningful drag on offense.
all_picks.case_against: Burrows' high HR/9 is the worry — if he gives up two early bombs to Buxton/Correa, the under math gets ugly fast. Also, Ryan has occasionally had short outings (the 4-IP, 5-ER nuker exists in his profile) — if he gets knocked around early, the floor caves in.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Ryan 6+ IP of 2 or fewer runs, Burrows giving up 3-4 across 5 innings, cold weather suppressing the HR ball.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Cold-weather game, ace vs. a guy with a 5.72. We've been here before.

all_picks.headline: Sale dominating in a park that swallows fly balls.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Chris Sale is having a vintage season — 1.96 ERA, 0.91 WHIP, 29.6% strikeout rate over 55 innings. Those numbers aren't a small-sample mirage; that's near-Cy Young pace and he's been remarkably efficient (18.3 IP per start across his last three, meaning he's going deep and saving the Atlanta bullpen). When the starter's WHIP is under 1.00, baserunners are scarce, and scarce baserunners means a low ceiling on the opposing scoring.

The Marlins counter with Janson Junk, who has actually been competent (4.14 ERA, 1.24 WHIP, decent 1.08 HR/9 in 50 innings). Not a stopper, but not a fire-starter either — he keeps the ball in the yard, which matters more in a park where Statcast HR factor is 0.86 (one of the most HR-suppressing venues in MLB). The roof at LoanDepot means no weather variance either.

The path to the under: Sale eats 7+ innings of one-run ball, Junk gives Atlanta 3-4 runs across 5-6 innings, and the bullpens close it out in a 4-2 or 3-2 type game. Combined K rate between these two starters suppresses the slate, and the park does the rest.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened 7.0 and stayed there across all three books — no movement, which means the market and the sharps agree this is a low-scoring spot. Under priced -102 to -105.
all_picks.weather_park: LoanDepot is indoor, HR factor 0.86, runs factor 0.94 — significant downward pressure on scoring.
all_picks.case_against: Sale's the variance here. If he lets a 1st-inning homer get out or his pitch count climbs early, Atlanta's pen (mostly fine but not elite) could leak runs late. And if the Braves jump on Junk early for 4-5 in the first three innings, the under is in trouble before Sale's even pitched a full game. Marlins offense is bad but not nonexistent.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Sale going 7+ giving up 0-2, Junk going 5-6 giving up 2-3, no bullpen meltdown.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Sale at 1.96 in a roof-closed pitcher's park. The line is 7. You do the math.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board today. Sale has been the most reliable arm in baseball this year, the park kills offense, and the price is right at even money. If anything's a 'show up and cash' play, it's this one.

autopsy.post_mortem: The under died on Janson Junk, not Chris Sale. Sale was excellent — 7 IP, 1 ER, 8 K — and held up his end of the thesis perfectly. But Junk was a fire-starter in disguise: he entered with a 6.35 ERA in May and had surrendered 7 runs over 5.2 innings to the Rays just five days earlier. The Braves tagged him for a 3-run Riley homer in the 2nd and a 3-run Smith homer in the 6th, putting up 8 runs off Junk alone. The model correctly identified Sale's side of the ledger but materially underweighted Junk's recent collapse — his 4.14 ERA was a season-long average masking a brutal May, and the 'case against' section even flagged the Braves jumping on Junk early as the primary risk, then didn't adjust the confidence or units accordingly.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When the opposing starter's current-month ERA diverges from his season ERA by 2+ runs (Junk: 4.14 season / 6.35 May), treat the under as structurally compromised and require the total to be set at least 0.5 below the implied run total before playing it — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One loss on a bad-month ERA divergence is noise; track over minimum 10 comparable starts before codifying.

all_picks.headline: Chase Burns at 1.87 ERA is the better pitcher in this matchup — and we're getting paid plus-money to back him.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Chase Burns is having a monster year: 1.87 ERA, 1.00 WHIP, 26.6% K rate, and 53 innings of consistent dominance. His ERA is over three runs lower than Jesús Luzardo's 5.07 on the other side. Luzardo's underlying stuff is good (28.6% K rate, 6.1% BB rate) but he's been getting hit hard — 5+ ERA over 49.2 innings is a real sample, not a fluke.

The market has the Reds at +125, which implies ~44% — we model them closer to 50% straight up, possibly slightly favored. Citizens Bank Park has a 1.10 HR factor, which is the one real concern (Burns has given up 1.02 HR/9), but Burns' overall command and strikeout profile keeps the damage contained even in a homer park. When you can take the better pitcher and get plus money on the moneyline, you take it — the dog price gives us a margin for variance that the favorite side doesn't get.
all_picks.the_market: Reds opened around +130 and have ticked down slightly to +124/+125 across the books — that's mild sharp support for the dog without any major movement.
all_picks.weather_park: Citizens Bank Park, outdoor, HR factor 1.10 — the main reason this isn't a higher-confidence play. One swing can change everything.
all_picks.case_against: Luzardo's strikeout stuff is real — if he has one of his good nights, he can shut Cincinnati down for six. The Phillies lineup at home in a homer park is the most dangerous offense Burns will face this month. A two-homer night flips this game and the dog ticket. We're betting on Burns being who his numbers say he is over a smaller-sample Luzardo bounceback.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Burns going 6+ innings of 2-or-fewer-run ball, Reds scratching 3-4 against Luzardo and a solid Phillies pen, and the homer math not blowing up in our face.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Better pitcher, plus money. That's the trade — we don't need to overthink the dog tag.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Big 15-game MLB board plus one NBA playoff game (Knicks-Cavs); pitching headliners include Cease, McLean, Burns, Misiorowski vs Brown, and Suarez at Kauffman against a Falter disaster start.
all_picks.executive_summary: Fifteen MLB games and a Knicks-Cavs playoff tilt headline a deep Tuesday board. The headline arm is Ranger Suarez walking into Kauffman against Bailey Falter's 10.13 ERA — that's the cleanest data mismatch on the slate. Behind it, Nolan McLean (2.92 ERA, 31.5% K) and Chase Burns (1.87 ERA) both check the boxes against beatable opposition. The ladder rides with the Red Sox — best floor by a mile, pitching edge so wide the market hasn't priced it cleanly yet.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Fifteen MLB games on the board with first pitches stretching from Miami at 4:10 ET to the West Coast night caps, plus a Knicks-Cavs playoff game at MSG. Pricing climate is normal — most totals sit at 7.5 to 8.5, most favorites in the -130 to -160 band, no obvious public traps screaming for action. Data confidence on the IntelPacks comes in at 0.4 across the board because lineups aren't confirmed yet, which means we have to lean on the cleanest pitching mismatches rather than offense-driven angles.

The board narrowed to six real candidates on first review. Suarez at Kauffman against Bailey Falter — Falter's 10.13 ERA and 1.7 IP/start are a five-alarm fire. McLean (2.92 ERA, 31.5% K rate, 0.69 HR/9) drawing Foster Griffin and the Nationals at home. Chase Burns at 1.87 ERA against Luzardo's 5.07 in Philly. Cease (2.41 ERA, 34.6% K, 0.17 HR/9 — absurd) against Will Warren and the Yankees. Ben Brown (1.60 ERA) vs Misiorowski (2.12, 39.6% K) at Wrigley as a potential under spot. And Messick (2.35 ERA, 28.3% K) against Keider Montero in Detroit.

What got cut: Cease ML at -136 is the right side but the juice is heavier than the edge — Yankees lineup is the best Cease will face in months and Warren's been solid at 3.42. Cubs-Brewers under 7.5 is tempting with two aces, but both bullpens have been shaky and Wrigley wind can flip on you — data_confidence too thin to commit. Burns is excellent but Luzardo's 28.6% K rate keeps the Phillies' over equation alive in a hitter's park (PF 1.10 HR); fade Phillies on the road wins, not at home. Messick vs Montero looks like a Guardians ML spot but -158 juice is right at our ceiling and Montero's 0.99 WHIP says he's not a giveaway. Knicks -7.5 is a playoff number we can't model with the data we have.

Final three: Red Sox ML at Kansas City as the headline pitching mismatch and ladder pick (best floor — Falter is essentially an opener masquerading as a starter, Suarez is a legitimate top-20 SP). Mets ML on the road against Washington — McLean's profile dwarfs Foster Griffin and the Mets are getting a price that implies they're closer to a coin flip than they should be. Reds +125 at Philly — Chase Burns is the better pitcher in this matchup and we're getting a plus-money dog, the rare spot where we get paid to take the right side.

all_picks.headline: Nolan McLean's 31.5% strikeout rate and 0.69 HR/9 walking into a Nationals lineup that hasn't been able to slow anyone down.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Nolan McLean is quietly putting together one of the best young-pitcher seasons in baseball — 2.92 ERA across 52.1 innings, 0.96 WHIP, a 31.5% strikeout rate that puts him in the top 10 in MLB, and a 0.69 HR/9 that means he's not getting beat by the longball. K% above 30% is the single most predictive stat for a pitcher staying dominant — strikeouts don't depend on defense or luck on balls in play. He's the real deal.

Forward-throwing Foster Griffin on the other side is fine but unspectacular — 3.53 ERA, 1.41 HR/9. That HR/9 number matters in a neutral park (Nationals Park PF 1.02) against a Mets lineup that has been running an above-average offense all season. The Mets are -142, implying ~58.6%; we model them closer to 63-65% given the pitching gap and the Nationals' overall roster quality. This is a quiet edge — no flashy line movement, just a better team with a better pitcher getting a fair price.
all_picks.the_market: Line opened Mets -140-ish and has held across all three books — no sharp move either way, which is consistent with the books pricing the matchup correctly but offering a small edge to the side with the better arm.
all_picks.weather_park: Nationals Park, outdoor, neutral run (1.01) and HR (1.02) factors — no environmental tilt.
all_picks.case_against: McLean is young — 52 innings is still a smallish sample for projecting forward, and rookies can hit walls. Foster Griffin pitches to contact and can string together quiet outings against teams that try to swing for the fences. If the Mets' bullpen has to close a one-run lead, the Nationals can grind out a late rally — they've done it before.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: McLean dominating into the 6th, Mets stacking 4+ on Griffin and the Nats pen, no late-inning leverage spot for Washington.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Strikeout rates don't lie. McLean punches out a third of the guys he sees — that travels anywhere.

Mets ML on the road against Washington — McLean's profile dwarfs Foster Griffin and the Mets are getting a price that implies they're closer to a coin flip than they should be. Reds +125 at Philly — Chase Burns is the better pitcher in this matchup and we're getting a plus-money dog, the rare spot where we get paid to take the right side.

autopsy.post_mortem: The Mets built a 5-0 lead and the thesis was playing out — then a once-in-a-generation play erased it. With the bases loaded in the 2nd, McLean's sweeper to James Wood glanced off debutant Nick Morabito's arm in left field and caromed into center, allowing Wood to score an inside-the-park grand slam — only the 9th such play since 1994. The Nationals then added three more in the 3rd on a homer, a passed ball, and a sac fly to go up 7-5. McLean's final line (9 R, 6 ER, 5.2 IP) was a career-worst, but three of those runs were unearned and the decisive blow was a freak defensive misplay on a ball that had no business leaving the infield. The pick was correctly reasoned; a ball bouncing off a rookie's arm in the 2nd inning is not a model failure.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; the inside-the-park grand slam is a ~0.1% frequency event and not a repeatable risk factor to model against.

all_picks.headline: OKC at home against a young Spurs team — six and a half is a reasonable number to lay with this talent gap.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Thunder are the better team across every meaningful dimension — talent, depth, defensive scheme, and home-court familiarity at Paycom. The market has them at -235 ML, which translates to roughly 68% win probability. The -6.5 spread implies they win by 7+ in something like 52-54% of their winning scenarios. With OKC's typical margin of victory profile against rebuilding-tier opponents at home, 6.5 is the right side.

San Antonio is dangerous for stretches because of their young core's talent, but they're not a team that wins road games against top-tier opponents by being efficient — they win them by getting hot. In a half-court game in a hostile building, the variance favors the favorite covering, not the underdog hanging around. OKC's defense in particular makes life miserable on possession-by-possession execution, which is exactly what punctures young road teams.

Line has been static at -6.5 across all three books, no key-number movement. That's a market that's comfortable, not one that's reaching.

the_data.label: Spread
the_data.context: no movement, all three books aligned

all_picks.the_market: Spread opened -6.5 and held across DK, FD, and MGM at -110 both ways. No sharp money trying to buy through the key 7. Market is settled here.
all_picks.case_against: Young teams playing loose on the road can hang for three quarters, and if SA hits some early threes the cover number becomes a problem fast. OKC also has a tendency to let off the gas with a comfortable lead, leading to backdoor covers — that's the real risk on any number 6+ against a team with shooters.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: OKC building a double-digit lead by halftime, defense suffocating Spurs' half-court sets, and the margin staying north of 7 down the stretch.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Talent gap at home, line hasn't moved, no need to dress it up.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Big 14-game MLB board with one NBA playoff matchup and one NHL game. Several elite pitchers in soft spots create clear under and favorite angles.
all_picks.executive_summary: Fourteen MLB games headlined by two of the most lopsided pitching mismatches of the week — McClanahan (2.27 ERA) hosting a sputtering Trevor Rogers in a dome that already kills offense, and Imanaga (2.32 ERA, 0.90 WHIP) drawing a wild Brandon Sproat at Wrigley. Add in OKC laying a manageable number at home against a Spurs team they should overwhelm, and we've got a clean three-pick day. Ladder rides with the Cubs in the parlay slot — best floor on the board.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Fifteen total games on the board: fourteen MLB, one NBA playoff (OKC-SA), one NHL (BUF-MTL). MLB pricing is fairly tight across the three books — DK, FD, and MGM are all within a couple cents on most lines — which actually makes the pitching-driven angles cleaner because we're not chasing soft numbers, we're betting clear talent gaps.

The candidates that rose during review: Rays-Orioles under 7.5 (McClanahan 2.27 ERA, 0.23 HR/9 vs Trevor Rogers at 5.77 ERA — but both being lefty starters in a dome screams under, not Rays ML); Cubs-Brewers (Imanaga 2.32 ERA, 0.90 WHIP vs Sproat 5.75 ERA, 13% BB rate); Yankees-Blue Jays (Weathers 3.00 ERA, 29.8 K% — but -170 juice exceeds our -150 cap on a straight); Tigers-Guardians (Framber Valdez at home, but Tigers ML priced -150 at best book, edge thin); Twins-Astros (both starters are walking machines with tiny samples — pass, too volatile); OKC-Spurs playoff spot (Thunder -6.5 at home, Spurs without their core in a hostile building); Dodgers-Padres total (two elite SPs in pitcher's park, but already priced to that reality at 7.5).

What got cut: Yankees ML at -170 fails the juice cap. Twins-Astros — Rojas has 7.1 IP and a 23.5% BB rate, Imai has 12.2 IP and 21.5% BB rate, sample sizes too thin to trust either way. Tigers ML — the edge over -150 implied probability is razor-thin, and Cecconi has actually been steady relative to his ERA. Dodgers-Padres under — market already sitting at 7.5, no edge to chase. Marlins-Braves — Ritchie's 16% walk rate is scary but Meyer's sample is also limited; data confidence below the floor.

The three survivors: Rays-Orioles under 7.5 (McClanahan dominance + Rogers regression + Tropicana suppressing), OKC -6.5 (rest, talent, home court in a closeout-type spot against a young Spurs team), and the ladder parlay anchored by Imanaga's Cubs plus the Rays under to land near even money. The ladder gets the parlay because Imanaga ML at -162 is too short for a straight ladder slot, but combining two of our highest-floor plays at +105 hits the band cleanly.

autopsy.post_mortem: This game was a double-overtime Western Conference Finals opener — OKC was not blown out. Per CBS Sports, the Spurs were up 10 with 9:10 left in regulation, OKC clawed back, and the lead changed hands twice with the game tied three times in under two minutes before the clock ran out on regulation. Wembanyama (41 pts, 24 reb) and Harper (24 pts, 7 steals) took over in double OT to seal it — the Thunder -6.5 cover was alive deep into the fourth quarter and was ultimately killed by two overtime periods, not by a bad thesis.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The pick was correctly reasoned — OKC led or was competitive through regulation — and was undone by a specific, low-probability double-OT outcome, not a structural model failure.

all_picks.headline: McClanahan in a dome, Rogers regressing, total opens and stays at 7.5 — the math is on the under.
all_picks.the_thesis:
This is the cleanest pitching mismatch on the board that still has a workable total. Shane McClanahan is sitting on a 2.27 ERA with a 0.98 WHIP and a microscopic 0.23 HR/9 — that last number is elite, top-five-in-baseball territory. He's striking out 25.8% with manageable walks at 9.4%. Translation: he's missing bats, limiting hard contact, and the ball isn't leaving the yard against him.

Trevor Rogers on the other side has been a disaster — 5.77 ERA, 1.54 WHIP, 1.32 HR/9. He's a lefty pitching in a dome where his fly-ball tendency is somewhat masked, but he's also been giving up traffic and burning his pitch count. The interesting wrinkle: when Rogers struggles, he struggles by walking guys and giving up the long ball, not by getting hammered for clusters of singles. That favors the under — fewer big innings of station-to-station damage, more solo shots that don't cluster.

Tropicana Field plays as a pitcher's park (0.95 run factor, 0.92 HR factor), and 7.5 is already a low number. We don't need a gem from both guys — we need McClanahan to do McClanahan things for 6 and Rogers to give up 3-4 instead of 6-7. Both bullpens are functional. The under cashes around 60-62% of the time in this specific setup.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and hasn't budged across all three books. No movement on a number this low against a 5.77 ERA pitcher suggests the market already knows McClanahan is the anchor — and sharp money isn't trying to push it lower because there's nowhere to go.
all_picks.weather_park: Tropicana Field is indoors, no weather. Park plays 0.95 runs / 0.92 HR — a real edge for the under.
all_picks.case_against: Rogers gets knocked out in the third, the Rays' offense rolls against Baltimore's middle relief, and we eat a 6-3 Rays win that pushes over 7.5. The under also gets cooked if McClanahan has one of his rare bad starts — he's an injury-history guy and one clunker is always in play. Lefty-on-lefty also doesn't fully neutralize Baltimore's righty bats, who can punish mistakes.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: McClanahan goes 6+ giving up 2 or fewer, Rogers gives up 3-4 over 4-5 innings, neither bullpen blows up.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Sometimes the edge is just one ace, one mess, and a dome. We don't have to overthink this one.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Big 14-game MLB board with one NBA playoff matchup and one NHL game. Several elite pitchers in soft spots create clear under and favorite angles.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis correctly identified Rogers as a liability but fatally misread HOW he damages you. The pick's own 'case against' warned Rogers hurts via solo shots and walks — not clusters of singles — yet Rogers sent 10 batters to the plate in a five-run second inning, exactly the station-to-station cluster damage the model said was unlikely. Tampa Bay then batted around again in a four-run sixth, capped by Caminero's three-run homer, pushing the total to 22 runs (TB 16, BAL 6) — nearly triple the 7.5 line. McClanahan did his job (held Baltimore scoreless into the third, extending his streak to 23⅔ innings), but the under was already dead by the end of the second inning on Rogers alone. The model's core error: it discounted the 'big inning via traffic' scenario for Rogers despite his 1.54 WHIP screaming exactly that risk.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a starter carries a WHIP above 1.45, do not lean Under regardless of the opposing ace — high-WHIP pitchers generate multi-run innings via baserunner accumulation at a rate that overwhelms even elite run suppression on the other side; flag any Under pick where one starter's WHIP exceeds 1.40 for manual review. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern.)
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One blowup from a high-WHIP starter does not validate a blanket filter — watch for recurrence across 10+ similar setups before codifying.

all_picks.headline: Peralta's a stable mid-rotation arm; the Yankees are throwing an opener with an 18% walk rate.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Freddy Peralta is the real starter in this game. 3.10 ERA, 1.22 WHIP, 23.8% K rate, 0.92 HR/9 across 49.1 innings — that's a reliable mid-rotation arm trending stable. Against him, the Yankees are running out Elmer Rodríguez, who in 8.2 MLB innings this year has put up a 2.08 WHIP and an 18.2% walk rate. Eight innings is a tiny sample, yes, but it tells you all you need to know about the role: he's a bullpen/opener arm being asked to navigate a top-half NL East lineup at home.

If Rodríguez throws 2-3 innings and the Yankees pen has to cover six, even a healthy Yankees pen is going to lose innings to a Mets lineup that's been productive. Peralta's been giving the Mets 5+ innings consistently, and the Mets' bullpen has been one of the more reliable units in the NL.

-120 is fair for a home favorite with that level of starter mismatch. The de-vigged implied at home ML is 52.4% — I have the Mets at ~58%. That's 5-6 cents of edge, right at the bar.
all_picks.the_market: Mets -120 across all three books, line hasn't moved off open. No sharp signal either way — this is a soft favorite spot the public hasn't latched onto.
all_picks.weather_park: Citi Field 83°F, 9mph wind blowing out to center boosts HR ~13% — slight tailwind for both offenses, but park HR factor of 0.91 mostly cancels it.
all_picks.case_against: Yankees bullpen could lock it down once Rodríguez exits — Yankees have quality arms and a deep pen. The Yankees lineup is also legit, and Peralta has had occasional command issues (9.0% BB%). If Aaron Judge sees three good fastballs over the plate, that's a 2-run swing. Tiny sample on Rodríguez means our worst-case (he's actually fine) is plausible.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Rodríguez exits after 3 innings having issued multiple walks, Mets get to the middle relief, Peralta navigates 6 strong, Mets bullpen closes.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: When the other team's starter has more walks than innings pitched, you take the price.
all_picks.ladder_note: Highest floor on the board: even-money price, Peralta is the most stable variable, and the starter mismatch doesn't require a specific run total — just a win. No weather variance dependency on the result, only on the size.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Quiet 15-game MLB Sunday with one NBA play-in style game; two ace-vs-ace pitching matchups headline the board and a season debut by Giolito creates a clean under spot.

all_picks.slate_analysis:
Fifteen MLB games and a single NBA game (DET-CLE) on the board. The MLB slate is pricing climate-normal — most favorites in the -120 to -160 band with no obvious chalk traps, and totals clustered between 7.5 and 10.5 (the Coors game). No NHL, no other major sports moving.

The candidates that earned a hard look: PIT-PHI under 7.5 (Skenes/Wheeler, both elite), SEA-SD under 7.5 (Kirby vs Giolito season debut), NYM-NYY Mets ML (Peralta vs an opener with 18.2% walk rate), DET-TOR under 7.5 (Gausman elite but Flaherty's 24% K rate offsets some), STL-KC over (19mph wind blowing out but Kolek has 10.2 IP — sample too thin), and ATL-BOS Braves ML (Bello at 6.46 ERA but -155 is steep with no edge after de-vig).

What got cut: STL-KC over — Kolek's 10.2 IP sample is below the threshold of reliable, and a wind-driven over without lineup confirmation is variance-chasing, not edge. ATL-BOS Braves ML — Holmes is only league-average (4.35 ERA) and -155 prices in the matchup edge; no daylight to the de-vigged market. DET-TOR under — Flaherty walks the world (15.9% BB%) and could put crooked numbers up himself; Gausman's edge alone isn't enough. COL-ARI total — Coors variance with a bad Lorenzen is a coinflip, not a pick. TB-MIA was workable but Rasmussen's 1.49 HR/9 in a small sample and confidence_data of 0.4 keeps it off the card.

The final three: Skenes/Wheeler under is the strongest signal on the board — two of the three best pitchers in the National League starting on the same mound. Kirby/Giolito under is a clean fade of a starter who hasn't pitched in the majors in eight months, in a pitcher's park. Mets ML is the ladder — Peralta's a stable 3.10 ERA arm against a Yankees opener with a 2.08 WHIP and 18.2% walk rate in 8.2 innings; even-money price, highest floor of the three because we don't need a specific run total to land.

all_picks.headline: Kirby at home in a pitcher's park against a starter who hasn't pitched in the majors in eight months.
all_picks.the_thesis:
George Kirby is doing Kirby things — 2.84 ERA, 1.16 WHIP, 0.63 HR/9 over 57 innings. That HR/9 is elite for a fly-ball pitcher in a fly-ball-suppressing park. T-Mobile Park grades as one of the most pitcher-friendly venues in the league (0.93 run factor, 0.93 HR factor).

The edge is on the other side though. 
This will be Giolito's first MLB game in nearly eight months, and he had an ERA of almost 5.00 across four minor league appearances this season.
 Eight months off, rehab numbers shaky, and now stepping into a Sunday night MLB start against an offense that just got Arozarena and Julio rolling. Even if Giolito gives San Diego five passable innings, that means a heavily worked Padres pen for the back half. The counterargument is that bullpen-heavy games tend to go over — but Seattle's offense ranks well below average and the Padres' pen is solid.

The contrarian note: 
As for Kirby, he's coming off an outing in which he threw a season-low of 5 IP but gave up his second-most hits (7) and baserunners (9) of the season. Kirby has also been lit up by current Padres batters, allowing a .318 average (14 for 44) versus them.
 That's real and I'm respecting it — that's why this is a 1-unit play and not a 1.5.
all_picks.the_market: Total at 7.5 across all books, no meaningful movement. BetMGM and DK have under at -110/-112. Public model at CBS has the over but that's predicated on Giolito blowing up, which is the consensus narrative — we're fading it.
all_picks.weather_park: Roof considerations aside, T-Mobile is a pitcher's park and weather isn't a factor at this venue tonight.
all_picks.case_against: Giolito could simply be sharp in his debut — he's a veteran with big-league pedigree, and rehab ERAs are notoriously unreliable indicators. Kirby has shown signs of regression in his last start and Padres hitters have historically owned him (.318 BA). If Giolito gives them five clean and Kirby coughs up a 3-run inning, this total clears in a hurry. Also, Padres have won 5 straight vs Seattle.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Giolito working short and the Padres pen managing it; Kirby giving 6 quality; combined score landing 3-4.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Eight months off and your first start back is on Sunday Night Baseball against a Gold Glove command artist. Good luck.

all_picks.headline: Two Cy Young arms, two struggling lineups, and a number that hasn't moved off 7.5.
all_picks.the_thesis:
This is the cleanest pitching matchup on the entire board. 
Philadelphia righty Zack Wheeler squares off against Pittsburgh righty and defending NL Cy Young winner Paul Skenes.
 Skenes is sitting on a 1.98 ERA, 0.64 WHIP, 30.3% K rate, and a 0.72 HR/9 — that last number means he gives up homers at a rate that ranks among the very best in baseball. 
Skenes is coming off an eight-scoreless-inning masterpiece against the Rockies in which he allowed just two hits and struck out 10.


On the other side, Wheeler isn't far behind. He's at 2.55 ERA, 0.93 WHIP, and a 0.37 HR/9 — that's elite home-run suppression. 
He's coming off a gem against the Red Sox on Tuesday, when he went 7 1/3 innings and allowed just one run.
 Even the bullpen narrative cuts the right way for the under tonight: 
Philly's pen has a league-best 2.76 xFIP in that same span, with a 2.30 ERA, and they also got a full day's rest yesterday.


The Pirates' pen is the only real wobble — it's been getting tagged lately — but Skenes is throwing complete-game-adjacent outings. He went 8 innings his last start and we only need him to give us 6-7 to lock this in. 
Skenes should rack up plenty of punchouts against a Philly lineup with the fifth-highest chase rate over the past two weeks, swinging at 37.1% of pitches outside the zone.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has stayed at 7.5 across all three books — sharp money is comfortable here. BetMGM at -125 is the best price; DK is -120, FD -122.
all_picks.weather_park: PNC Park 78°F, 9mph wind blowing out to left — that's the only real concern, but both pitchers suppress contact hard enough to neutralize it.
all_picks.case_against: Wind blowing out 9mph at PNC could turn one Schwarber or O'Hearn swing into a 3-run inning that we never recover from. Skenes had one bad start this year — Opening Day vs the Mets where he allowed 5 runs in 0.2 IP. If he's off, the Phillies have the bats to make him pay. Wheeler is also five starts removed from thoracic outlet surgery and his velocity is down from last year (94.7 vs 96.1). Bullpens matter too — Pirates' pen has been awful lately.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Skenes and Wheeler combining for 13+ innings, both lineups failing to string anything together, and the under landing in the 5-6 run range.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two aces, no defense lawyer needed. The number hasn't moved an inch — that tells you everything.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Quiet 15-game MLB Sunday with one NBA play-in style game; two ace-vs-ace pitching matchups headline the board and a season debut by Giolito creates a clean under spot.

DET-TOR under — Flaherty walks the world (15.9% BB%) and could put crooked numbers up himself; Gausman's edge alone isn't enough. COL-ARI total — Coors variance with a bad Lorenzen is a coinflip, not a pick. TB-MIA was workable but Rasmussen's 1.49 HR/9 in a small sample and confidence_data of 0.4 keeps it off the card.

The final three: Skenes/Wheeler under is the strongest signal on the board — two of the three best pitchers in the National League starting on the same mound. Kirby/Giolito under is a clean fade of a starter who hasn't pitched in the majors in eight months, in a pitcher's park. Mets ML is the ladder — Peralta's a stable 3.10 ERA arm against a Yankees opener with a 2.08 WHIP and 18.2% walk rate in 8.2 innings; even-money price, highest floor of the three because we don't need a specific run total to land.

all_picks.headline: Rahm two back on a firm Donald Ross track with two major wins on his resume and the shortest-priced never-was sitting in front of him.
all_picks.the_thesis:
The setup screams Rahm. 
The morning wave took advantage of lighter winds while afternoon leaders faced whipping wind and firming greens
, which means the course is playing crusty into Sunday. 
Sunday's final round features the hottest temperatures with up to 83F and winds from the west at 8-12mph with gusts up to 20mph
 — that bakes greens further. 
It's a Donald Ross design on the outskirts of Philadelphia, a tricky parkland layout that plays to a par 70
. Par-70 Ross designs reward elite iron play and short-game scrambling — Rahm's bread and butter.

The leaderboard is the second piece. Smalley at +485 has never won on Tour and is making his major-contender debut in the final pairing two days in a row — that's a ton of pressure on a guy who admitted he doesn't love the spotlight. 
He spoke on Saturday about not handling the spotlight well
. Rahm tees off two back with two major closures on his resume (2021 U.S. Open, 2023 Masters). If Smalley wobbles early — and history says first-time major leaders wobble more than they hold — the door swings open to a chase pack of about six guys, and Rahm is the most proven closer in that group.

Åberg at +650 is the next-best price but he's 
a Swede who has struggled to close
. McIlroy at +760 is one shot further back. Rahm is the best price-to-position bet on this board.

all_picks.the_market: +550 at FanDuel is the high across DK/FD/MGM (DK +465, MGM +500). Implied 15.4% at FD vs 17.7% at DK. My fair line on Rahm given position, closing resume, course fit, and leader profile: ~21-22%. Edge of roughly 6 cents in implied probability. Take FanDuel.
all_picks.weather_park:
Aronimink (par 70, Donald Ross): Sunday mid-80s, W winds 8-12mph with gusts to 20. 
Gusts will reach the low 20s for the final round, but it will calm down a bit after the leaders tee off
. Firm and crusty after three days of sun and wind — favors elite iron players and scramblers. Pure Rahm setup.
all_picks.case_against:
He's still two back of a guy who just gutted out one of the toughest rounds of his life. 
22 players within four shots
 means even a Rahm 66 might not be enough if two or three guys in the chase pack also go low. And Rahm at majors hasn't been quite vintage Rahm the last 18 months — he's a closer, but he hasn't actually closed a major since the 2023 Masters.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Smalley plays the front nine like a first-time major leader plays the front nine — a couple of early bogeys, lead shrinks to one. Rahm makes 2-3 birdies on the front. By the turn it's a tie or Rahm has the lead. Then his Sunday experience does the rest while the rest of the chase pack (Rai, Taylor, Schmid) feels the major pressure they've never felt either.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Smalley at +485 is the market betting that a guy who's never been here before doesn't blink. Rahm at +550 is the market giving you a discount on a guy who's already won two of these. I'll take the discount.
all_picks.executive_summary:
Sunday at Aronimink and it's a Royal Rumble — 
22 players within four shots of the lead, 30 within five
. 
Alex Smalley leads at -6 with Rahm, Åberg, Rai, Taylor, Schmid at -4 and McIlroy/Schauffele/Reed all within three
. Smalley has never won on Tour and has never contended in a major — his +485 is the squeeze price on the board. The value lives one tier down with a guy who's done this before. Jon Rahm at +550 (FanDuel), two back, firm-and-fast conditions, established Sunday closer.

all_picks.headline: Nick Martinez is having a season, Alcantara is back to being Alcantara, and the dome takes weather out of the equation.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Nick Martinez is sitting at a 1.70 ERA with a 1.05 WHIP and a 5.2% walk rate — the latter is the key number for an under bet. Pitchers who don't walk people don't blow up. He's not a huge strikeout guy (16.1 K%), but he keeps the ball in the yard (0.57 HR/9) and gets you deep into games (15.7 IP/start in his last three).

Sandy Alcantara at 3.90 isn't his Cy Young self but he's back to being a workhorse — 19 IP per start in his last three, which is borderline absurd in 2026. He's at 0.63 HR/9 with a manageable walk rate. The combined HR/9 here is 1.20 across both starters, and we're playing in a dome at Tropicana with a 0.95 runs factor and 0.92 HR factor.

This is just a low-event game in a low-event environment with two starters who go deep and don't beat themselves.
